title: The Efficient Allocation of Consumption Under Moral Hazard and Hidden Access to the Credit Market
journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
summary_en: Insurance contracts work differently when the insured person can also borrow privately without the insurer knowing. This paper characterises efficient risk-sharing contracts when there are both moral hazard (the insured takes unobservable actions) and hidden access to credit markets. The hidden-credit-market feature tightens what the insurer can achieve: the optimal contract must be robust to the insured smuggling consumption forward using private borrowing. The paper derives the efficient allocation and shows how classical results change. A technical contribution to contract theory with applications to health insurance, unemployment insurance, and pension design.
policy_relevance: Relevant to any Hungarian social-insurance design where private credit access is a real margin: unemployment insurance (where beneficiaries can borrow), health insurance out-of-pocket coverage, retirement savings. Implication: optimal contract design must account for this hidden channel, and standard rule-of-thumb designs may be suboptimal. Caveat: theoretical; specific Hungarian implications require empirical estimates of the extent of private-credit substitution in each social-insurance program.
